Brand messages that express certainty increase consumer engagement on social media by making brands seem more powerful, with the effect being stronger among consumers with higher power distance beliefs.

The network of political retweets on Twitter exhibits a highly segregated partisan structure with limited connectivity between left- and right-leaning users, while the user-to-user mention network is dominated by a politically heterogeneous cluster where ideologically-opposed individuals interact more frequently. This suggests that politically motivated individuals provoke interaction by injecting partisan content into information streams primarily aimed at ideologically-opposed users.
Users on Twitter post links to more politically moderate news sources than those they receive, despite a perception of polarization due to a small core of active users exhibiting polarization.

The study addresses the challenges of measuring word-of-mouth communication and demonstrates that online conversations can effectively measure this communication, with the dispersion of conversations across communities having explanatory power in predicting TV ratings.

Weak ties are more influential than strong ties in the interpretation and persuasiveness of consensus language, as weak ties evoke perceptions of a larger and more diverse group, signaling greater validity for the issue at hand.

The study finds that Trump's Twitter posts are more often controversial and subject to enduring engagement compared to Obama's, as evidenced by the differences in retweet-to-reply ratios and the dynamics of public engagement during the 2016 election period.

Negative information about a celebrity results in a decline in attitude toward the endorsed brand, moderated by association set size, timing of the negative information, and strength of the link between brand and celebrity.

Laypeople's trust ratings can effectively differentiate between lower- and higher-quality news sources, and incorporating these ratings into social media algorithms may reduce the spread of misinformation.
Posting about identity-relevant products on social media reduces subsequent purchase intentions, fulfilling identity-signaling needs, but moderators can mitigate this negative effect.

The echoverse has transformed brand communications, with online word of mouth leading to a negativity spiral, and companies benefit from using social media for personalized customer responses while still needing traditional communication methods.

The joint effect of tie strength and image-impairment concern on negative word-of-mouth (NWOM) transmission differs for males and females, with self- versus other-focused thoughts mediating this effect.
Users' online content generation activity has a causal effect on their social ties, and this feedback generates a multiplier effect on interventions that subsidize tie formation.

Users' reactions to virtual influencers on social media are influenced by the number and types of social actors present in the content, with users using more social words and 3rd-person pronouns when multiple social actors are involved, and more positive and anxious words when virtual influencers are presented alongside humans.

Sociodemographic factors propagate online social contagion, suggesting that social media campaign success relies on sociodemographic segmentation-based targeting, while geographic proximity does not significantly govern the spread of online conversations.
Long-term relationships in marketing services have a negative impact on service use, which dampens the impact of trust, and certain "dark side" constructs mediate this relationship.

This article reviews the literature on consumer engagement in social media and proposes a framework for future research, highlighting the lack of consensus on the definition of consumer engagement and its implications for research and practice.

The study examines the effect of social presence on consumer trust in e-services, finding that social presence affects consumer trust and that trust has a stronger effect on purchase intentions than Technology Acceptance Model beliefs.

The ideological segregation of online news consumption is low in absolute terms, higher than offline news consumption, and significantly lower than face-to-face interactions, with no evidence that the Internet is becoming more segregated over time.

Communicative messages aimed at changing anti-social or unhealthy behaviors can lead to a boomerang effect, where the intended message either fails to change attitudes or even increases the targeted negative behaviors, through two distinct processing paths.

The latent personality traits of online users accentuate the effectiveness of word-of-mouth (WOM) in social media platforms, with personality similarity between users positively influencing the likelihood of a subsequent purchase after exposure to WOM messages.

Consumers use protest framing tactics (injustice, identity, and agency) to mobilize mass audiences against firms, demonstrating their power to influence others and affirming their identities through the construction of complaint web sites.

The effects of emotional disclosure are equivalent whether participants think they are disclosing to a chatbot or to a person, supporting media equivalency as a primary mechanism for the consequences of disclosing to a chatbot.

Consumers who are personally similar to others in online word of mouth mimic positive emotion and social word use, while those similar in status mimic cognitive and descriptive word use, driven by affiliation versus achievement goals, respectively, affecting their engagement in posting incidence and volume.

The presence of reciprocity in Twitter follows can be explained by the phenomenon of homophily, and TwitterRank, an extension of the PageRank algorithm, effectively measures user influence by considering both topical similarity and link structure, outperforming existing algorithms.
Offline television advertising causes a short but significant positive effect on online chatter, with a stronger impact on visibility and virality compared to popularity and negativity.
Gender moderates the effect of managerially controlled trust influencers in the trust formation process online.

Social media was an important source of election news, with a significant impact on the consumption and belief in fake news stories, particularly those favoring a preferred candidate, especially in ideologically segregated networks.

This research identifies that content features like URLs and hashtags, as well as contextual features such as the number of followers and followees, significantly affect the retweetability of tweets, while the number of past tweets does not predict retweetability.

People direct hostility toward a transgressing in-group whose actions threaten their self-image and evoke collective shame, with collective shame mediating the relationship between in-group transgression and in-group-directed hostility.

The study characterizes the language use of Twitter users disclosing mental illnesses, finding that language-derived personality and demographic estimates can effectively distinguish users tweeting about depression or PTSD, with age being a significant predictor.

Committed consumers exhibit negative responses to in-contract transgressions due to lower trust in the brand, while violations of any aspect of the psychological contract result in negative or indifferent responses depending on the type of transgression.
Media frames significantly impact consumer judgments of legitimacy, with shifts in framing leading to changes in implicit associations and differing responses between users and nonusers.

Grandiose narcissism is positively related to time spent on social media, frequency of status updates, number of friends/followers, and frequency of posting selfies, while vulnerable narcissism is not significantly related to social media use, with culture and social media platform moderating these relationships.

This article investigates the impact of six forms of negatively valenced influencing behaviour (NVIB) on negative customer engagement behaviour (CEB) in online networks, focusing on how intensity levels of NVIB are moderated by positive reviews, suggesting that managers should use semantic tools to detect and prioritize handling more intense NVIBs.

Brand fan attacks on rival brands on social media induce broader brand engagement and have specific managerial control implications, with brand managers able to stimulate positive outcomes from rival fan postings.

The role of social networks in online information diffusion is significant, with weak ties being more responsible for the propagation of novel information than strong ties, despite stronger ties being individually more influential.

given_idea: Higher similarity in brand name translations leads to higher Chinese box office revenue, with the effect being stronger for movies that perform better in the home market, and higher informativeness in translated titles also increases box office revenue, particularly for movies with a greater cultural gap in the Chinese market. Both effects are strongest at the time of release and diminish over time.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: The effectiveness of brand name translation in multilingual international markets depends on the emphasis of the original English name versus the Chinese name and the method of translation used previously for brand names within the same category.

The study finds that Chinese consumers prefer vehicle models with semantic brand names, leading to 7.64% more sales compared to alphanumeric names, while showing the least preference for phonosemantic names, resulting in 4.92% lower sales than alphanumeric names; domestic firms benefit from semantic names, while foreign firms gain from foreign-sounding names, with entry-level products performing better with semantic names and high-end products excelling with foreign-sounding names.

Economic and cultural factors significantly influence the box-office performance of foreign films in Singapore, with films from countries with larger domestic markets and those culturally similar to Singapore performing better.

The majority of Fortune-500 companies localize their brand names in China and Hong Kong, primarily using transliteration, and they successfully avoid brand name mistakes while enhancing brand names with cultural symbols and positive connotations.

The cultural context significantly impacts the box office performance of U.S. films in foreign markets, with films performing better in countries that are culturally closer to the United States and those that have a greater degree of Americanization.

Foreign branding triggers cultural stereotypes that influence product perceptions and attitudes, with the French pronunciation of a brand name affecting perceived hedonism, brand attitudes, and brand name attitudes; congruent country-of-origin information does not enhance hedonic perceptions, while incongruence diminishes the effect. Despite direct sensory experience, consumer perceptions change due to French branding.

Proficient bilingual consumers prefer sound translation when the English name is emphasized but meaning translation when the Chinese name is emphasized, while less proficient bilinguals engage in semantic processing of dual names.

Chinese consumers perceive alpha-numeric brand names with lucky letters and numbers as luckier, of better quality, and more successful than those with unlucky letters and numbers, with lucky numbers being more favorably perceived than lucky letters.

Chinese brand attitudes are primarily affected by the match between script associations and brand associations, while brand attitudes of English names are primarily affected by the match between sound associations and brand associations.

Structural differences between Chinese and English affect mental representations, which influence consumer memory of verbal information, leading to differential effects on unaided brand recall and recognition based on language and mode of learning (auditory vs. visual).
The linguistic content of brand names in China influences product success, and understanding the preferred structures can guide marketers in creating effective brand names.

This paper develops a conceptual framework to understand the success of US movies in foreign markets, particularly focusing on the differing impacts of various factors on movie success in the US and Germany.

This study argues that cultural discount and cross-culture predictability of financial performance are quantitative manifestations of local reception, and analyzes whether Hollywood movies of different genres are more or less subject to cultural discount and lack of predictability when they travel to Hong Kong, revealing that comedies are highly particularistic while science fiction is the most universal.

The performance ("demand") and availability ("supply") of sequentially released products in international markets are interdependent, with movie attributes and advertising expenditures influencing revenues indirectly through their impact on exhibitors' screen allocations, and the time lag between releases weakening the relationship between domestic and foreign market performance.

The presence of a movie star has a stronger effect on theater allocations than movie characteristics, while the revenue difference is attributed to the characteristics of the star and nonstar movies, indicating that star effects on revenue come indirectly through theater allocations and movie characteristics.
Word-of-mouth activities significantly influence box office revenue, particularly in the early weeks after a movie's release, with the volume of word-of-mouth being more impactful than its valence.

Audience heterogeneity influences the effectiveness of commercial performance and artistic acclaim as market signals in international film markets, with their effects depending on cultural distance and distribution type.

The results support the hypothesized linkages between extrinsic cues and consumers' perceptions of quality, sacrifice, and value, indicating that the effects of price, brand name, store name, and country of origin on perceived quality are mediated by perceived sacrifice and perceived value.

The involvement of stars affects movies' expected theatrical revenues, with estimates suggesting that stars are worth approximately $3 million in theatrical revenues, and the impact of a newly recruited star is greater when the cast already has a strong track record. However, stars do not increase the valuation of film companies that release the movies, indicating they do not add more value than they capture.

The study indicates that actors have a direct effect and directors an indirect effect on consumers' movie choice, while the number of opening week screens is overestimated in traditional models, and releasing a movie against others of the same genre adversely affects box-office performance.

High early movie revenues enhance subsequent movie ratings, high advertising spending on movies supported by high ratings maximizes revenues, sequel movies tend to reap more revenues but receive lower ratings than originals, viewers' viewing and rating histories and movie communities' collective opinions explain viewer satisfaction, various aspects of ratings explain viewers' new movie ratings, and viewers' movie experiences can cause them to become more critical in ratings over time. There is also a U-shaped relationship between viewers' genre preferences and genre-specific movie ratings for heavy viewers.

The study finds that price sensitivity is lower when a brand is positioned at the upper price-quality boundary, when only brand names are provided instead of quality ratings, and when quality information is added to available brand-name information.

The performance of movies at the box office is significantly influenced by prerelease blog volume and advertising on opening day, while postrelease performance is affected by postrelease blog valence and advertising, with variations across different demographic characteristics and geographic markets.

Domestic brands in emerging markets can improve their country-of-origin image by dissociating from negative stereotypes and pretending to be foreign, which may lead to a common good problem where high-quality firms prevent low-quality peers from free-riding on their reputation.

This research provides empirical generalizations in managing the supply and demand of motion pictures, addressing the endogeneity of box office and screen allocation while analyzing a comprehensive sample of movies released in the US and Germany.

The study hypothesizes that movie piracy has both a cannibalization effect that reduces legitimate sales and a promotional effect that increases word-of-mouth and stimulates sales, with the negative effects from cannibalization outweighing the positive promotional benefits. If piracy were eliminated, box-office revenues would increase significantly.

Exposure to a brand name with sound repetition in its phonetic structure produces positive affect, which favorably affects consumers' brand evaluations, reactions to cross-selling, and product choice, moderated by consumers' sensitivity to repetition, opportunity to experience emotions, and deviation from linguistic expectations.

Online word of mouth and expert reviews significantly impact box office revenues, with frequency of online WOM and valence rating of expert reviews being significant factors in the U.S. domestic market, while only the frequency of online WOM is significant in international markets.

The sequence of movie releases significantly influences the dilution and enhancement of celebrity brands, with evidence supporting the existence of these effects on brand equity, while star favorability erodes over time.

Genre taste dissimilarities are positively related to cultural distance between countries and negatively related to the English proficiency of the importing country, while economic attributes of the importer have no effect on taste dissimilarity. Additionally, the genre tastes of individual countries have converged toward those of American audiences during the years 2002-2007.
Brand names influence the demand for twin automobiles by affecting consumers' perceptions of quality and prestige, leading to differing relative prices among models perceived as substitutes.

High similarity between a core product and an extension product is only beneficial for brand evaluations when consumers evaluate the brand favorably, and additional information can affect consumers' similarity perceptions without changing the extension product significantly.
Firms adapt their brand names in response to increased market structure factors, and more standardized brand names lead to higher cost savings and sales volume.

The features of alpha-numeric brand names, such as the magnitude of the number, its shape or spoken sound, and the symbolism of the words or letters, significantly influence consumer perceptions and expectations of the product.
Consumers from Eastern cultures perceive higher brand extension fit and evaluate brand extensions more favorably than do Western consumers, with holistic thinking driving these cultural differences.

Brand names composed of low frequency words stimulate distinctive encoding, which may eliminate or reverse the pattern of effects observed with high frequency words, where memory for brand information is inversely related to the number of related associations.

The study conceptualizes and measures the monetary value of brand extension rights in motion pictures, demonstrating that variations in key extension product attributes, such as the continued participation of stars, significantly affect this value through both forward and reciprocal spillover effects.

Auditory elements interfere more with the learning of and cognitive responding to English ad copy than with Chinese ad copy, while visual elements have the opposite effect; auditory elements serve as better retrieval cues for English ad copy, and visual elements serve as better retrieval cues for Chinese ad copy.
The observed seasonality of box-office revenues is influenced by both the underlying demand for movies and the timing of the release of major films, with price rigidities amplifying this effect.

Critics influence and predict box office revenue, with negative reviews having a greater detrimental effect than positive reviews, particularly in the first week of a film's release, and the impact of reviews is moderated by star power and budgets.

Consumer expertise and attribute strength moderate the effects of country of origin on product evaluations, with experts relying on attribute strength when information is unambiguous and novices relying on country of origin, while both groups use country of origin differently when information is ambiguous.

In developing countries, brands perceived as having a nonlocal country of origin are preferred over local brands due to perceived quality and social status, with the effect being stronger for consumers who admire developed country lifestyles, are susceptible to normative influence, and for product categories with high social signaling value.

Consumers are more likely to contribute online reviews for very obscure movies and very high-grossing movies, leading to a U-shaped relationship between the propensity to review and box office revenues.

Linguistic differences between Chinese and English qualitatively affect the processing of verbal information, with English words leading to superior temporal memory and Chinese words leading to superior interitem associative memory, absent for pictorial information.

The presence of linguistic features in brand names positively influences brand-name memory, particularly for less familiar brands, with specific linguistic variables such as semantic appositeness, paranomasia, initial plosives, unusual spelling, and blending showing varying effects based on brand familiarity.

Brand extensions capture greater market share and realize greater advertising efficiency than individual brands, with the strength of the parent brand positively related to market share but not affecting advertising efficiency; advertising efficiency is elevated when similarity is high based on intrinsic attributes, and market share effects are elevated in markets with few competitors.

The proposed methodology predicts box office revenues more accurately (29 percent lower mean squared error (MSE)) compared to benchmark methods, using textual features extracted from movie scripts and estimated production budgets.

The relationships between price and perceived quality and between brand name and perceived quality are positive and statistically significant, while the positive effect of store name on perceived quality is small and not statistically significant; the type of experimental design and the strength of price manipulation significantly influence the observed effect of price on perceived quality.

The marketing support received by a movie interacts with the direction and magnitude of its excess stock return post-launch, indicating that movies with above-average prelaunch advertising have lower postlaunch stock returns than those with below-average advertising, and that high media support can lead to a lowering of stock price for box office hits due to high performance expectations built up prior to launch.

The choice of advertising language affects advertising effectiveness for multinational corporations (MNCs) but not local companies, and different language formats vary in their effectiveness for different types of products.

given_idea: People are more likely to endorse markets and lines for allocating scarce goods when preferences are dissimilar, as these systems help sort preferences; conversely, they react negatively when such allocation rules are used in situations where preferences are similar.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: People believe resources systematically differ according to how well they signal preferences, and allocation policies seem fairer if based on resources that clearly signal preferences.
Individual preferences for redistribution are negatively affected by social mobility, and perceptions of fairness in the mobility process influence attitudes towards redistribution.
This study documents a different kind of preference reversal in a riskless setting, challenging the notion of consistent and stable preferences in utility maximization.

Consumers take on "requestor" or "responder" roles in joint consumption decisions, leading to a preference expression asymmetry where requestors desire preference expressions while responders do not, resulting in preference and social friction costs for responders.

The data are inconsistent with preference theory, suggesting that no optimization principles underlie human choices, and that uniformities in choice behavior may arise from different principles than those generally accepted in economics.

People's preferences are often constructed during the elicitation process, influenced by various contextual factors, leading to preference reversals that challenge traditional notions of rational choice.

The preference reversal phenomenon indicates that decisionmakers do not have a fixed preference order, as different elicitation methods can alter the relative weighting of attributes and lead to varying preference orderings.
The theory posits that losses and disadvantages exert a greater influence on consumer preferences than gains and advantages, leading to preference reversals based on changes in the reference point.

Individuals' risk preferences are not stable across different institutions, as evidenced by their risk-loving behavior in English clock auctions and risk-averse behavior in first-price auctions, with statistically different risk coefficients across institutions.

Consumers create consideration sets based on two macro-level motives: the preference for easy-to-compare alternatives and the likelihood of containing an optimal alternative, which are influenced by the need to make trade-offs between benefits.

Consumers complicate their choices by increasing choice conflict through overweighting small disadvantages of superior alternatives, converging overall evaluations, reversing preference ordering, and choosing less preferred alternatives, particularly when they anticipate a decision to be easier than expected, leading to an artificial increase in effort.

Risk-neutral resource users will always prefer lottery allocation to price allocation, and the efficient capacity for lottery rationing exceeds that for price allocation. Objectives may be better met by joint use of allocation mechanisms.

Consumer preference for retailers offering larger assortments decreases as the attractiveness of the options in their assortments increases, potentially leading to a reversal of preferences in favor of retailers offering smaller assortments, with the relationship being concave such that the marginal impact of assortment size on choice decreases as option attractiveness increases.

The tendency to prefer an alternative is influenced by the context of tradeoffs within the set, with tradeoff contrast enhancing or hindering preference, and extremeness aversion enhancing attractiveness for intermediate options while diminishing it for extreme options.

When the budget contracts, consumers select less variety compared to when their budget expands to the same level, driven by a desire to avoid feelings of loss associated with allocation cuts across many items.
The equilibrium signaling is more informative when agents' preferences are more similar, and the equilibrium with the greatest number of elements is Pareto-superior to all other equilibria.
Buyer confusion increases market prices, as evidenced by higher prices and profits when the number of attributes of goods is higher, leading to more suboptimal choices by buyers.
This literature review suggests that individuals make choices among alternatives based on rational behavior, maximizing utility or expected utility, and that their preferences are transitive.

People show more relative preference for environmental improvements in selling modes than they do in buying modes, indicating a preference reversal that is greater for environmental changes compared to market items.

The decision to defer choice is influenced by the absolute difference in attractiveness among alternatives, and this effect decreases when subjects practice making monetary trade-offs among the available alternatives.

Recommendation signage can hinder consumer choice for those with developed preferences by increasing decision complexity and leading to larger consideration sets, while these effects are mitigated for consumers with less developed preferences and smaller assortments.

An increase in market size leads to preference externalities that enhance consumer satisfaction within demographic groups, but these benefits are diminished or negative across different demographic groups, resulting in smaller groups receiving less variety from the market.

Laypeople's misperceptions of inequality, particularly their underestimation of its extent, influence their attitudes toward redistribution and are driven by local environmental cues, but correcting these misperceptions can positively affect preferences and policy outcomes.
People will tend to select the first option in a set when choosing between generally undesirable options, and will tend to select the last when choosing between generally desirable options.
Consumers prefer scarce products even when scarcity is due to others' previous purchases, and this preference is influenced by the perceived uniqueness and spatial distance of others.

Preference elicitation procedures, specifically willingness to pay (WTP) and choice, are associated with different expressed preferences, where choices favor hedonic goods and WTP favors utilitarian goods, influenced by deliberation and affect heuristics.

Consumers exhibit heterogeneous search costs and preferences, leading to choices that do not always align with the lowest price, influenced by factors such as seller reputation and advert characteristics.
If a fraction of people exhibits inequality aversion, it can explain the puzzling evidence of cooperation and competition in bargaining situations.

A high construal of a choice decreases comparative trade-offs relative to a low construal, decreasing the compromise and background-contrast effects and increasing the attraction effect by reducing attribute-level trade-offs.

Consumers with inequity aversion experience psychological disutility when buying products at unfair prices, leading to nonmonotone optimal quality decisions by firms, and stronger inequity aversion can lower consumer surplus while potentially benefiting efficient firms.
Feeding America’s switch to a market-based mechanism for food allocation allows food banks to prioritize their preferences more effectively than the previous centralized queuing system.

The need to choose one target among multiple needy targets hinders donations, leading to higher opt-out rates when donors are presented with multiple options compared to a single target, and this effect is driven by the conflict between the wish to be helpful and the wish to be fair.

Altruistic and selfish consumers achieve better alignment with their individual preferences in joint decisions when partnered with someone of the opposite interpersonal orientation (heterogeneous dyad) compared to a matching partner (homogeneous dyad), due to reduced negotiation in heterogeneous dyads.
People like to be perceived as fair, which influences their behavior in economic environments, particularly in the context of the 50-50 division norm.

The need for justifying hedonic consumption drives choice patterns where preferences between hedonic and utilitarian goods can reverse based on presentation, with hedonic alternatives rated higher when presented singly, but utilitarian alternatives chosen when presented jointly, and preferences for expending time and money differ between hedonic and utilitarian items.
A reduction in search costs raises market prices, and preference diversity and search frictions affect market prices and equilibrium.

People avoid monetary trade-offs associated with loved ones when purchasing symbolic items, leading them to choose higher-priced options, search less for lower-priced items, and negotiate less, resulting in greater spending than if they engaged in cost-benefit reasoning.
People expect others to like similar products but erroneously believe that others dislike dissimilar ones, driven by a belief that others have narrow and homogeneous ranges of preferences.

Consumers' choices are influenced by salient attributes, leading them to favor goods with higher quality/price ratios, and this model explains various phenomena such as decoy effects and context-dependent willingness to pay.

The cancellation and focus model predicts that features shared by choice alternatives are canceled, leading to higher evaluations of alternatives judged from unique-good pairs compared to unique-bad pairs, and that the relative preference of the subject is influenced by the manner in which alternatives are presented (sequentially or side by side).
The overall perception of scarcity increases the choice share of the most-preferred item from a product class by inducing arousal, which polarizes evaluations of individual items in the choice set.

Ambiguity aversion drives a preference for established brands in multiattribute choices among branded alternatives, with this preference enhanced when ambiguity aversion is made more salient and when subjects anticipate evaluations from others.

The choice between two-dimensional options is more lexicographic than matching, indicating that the weighting of input is enhanced by their compatibility with the output, as demonstrated by the contingent weighting model applied to various compatibility effects.

This paper develops a unified framework for behavioral welfare economics that extends standard welfare analysis to accommodate situations where individuals make inconsistent choices, reflecting the inadequacy of the standard model of consumer decision making in capturing human behavior.

The effect of attribute framing on choice influences preferences for environmental costs differently among political affiliations, with Republicans and Independents showing a change in preferences when charges are labeled as a tax or an offset, while Democrats' preferences remain unaffected.

Presenting common features of choice alternatives increases the choice probability of the alternative expected to perform worse on that feature, due to consumers' expectations about those features, and this effect is influenced by four boundary conditions.

Benefit-based assortment organization leads to more abstract construal and heightens similarity perceptions among items, resulting in consumers selecting lower-priced items and being similarly satisfied with their top choice as with a lower-ranked option. Additionally, consumers' internal shopping objectives may interact with external organization to heighten similarity perceptions.
The relationship between individual choice and group opinion is nonmonotonic and is moderated by the degree of unanimity around an alternative, with choice patterns being curvilinear.

The measurement of loss aversion in empirical applications of the reference-dependent choice model is confounded by unaccounted-for heterogeneity in consumer price responsiveness, leading to biased estimates of loss aversion that are frequently lower and nonsignificant when accounting for this heterogeneity.

Lower-income individuals are held to more restrictive standards of permissible consumption, judged negatively for purchasing the same items as their higher-income peers, and this inequality in socially permissible consumption is influenced by perceptions of necessity.

given_idea: Researchers introduce a pioneering branding approach that integrates the diverse aspects of brand identity, co-creation, and brand governance in the era of hyperconnectivity.
original: The study proposes a new branding paradigm that accounts for the roles and functions of brands, the co-creation of brand value, and the management of brands in a hyperconnected world.

top_k.sentence: The study proposes a new branding paradigm that accounts for the roles and functions of brands, the co-creation of brand value, and the management of brands in a hyperconnected world.

A coherent brand architecture, facilitated by the brand relationship spectrum, can lead to impact, clarity, synergy, and leverage in brand management, addressing the challenges posed by market fragmentation and changing business environments.

This paper identifies influential work in branding, highlights learnings on brand positioning, integration, equity measurement, growth, and management, and outlines research gaps and questions in branding and brand equity.

The systematic model presented explains how iconic brands create "identity myths" that soothe collective anxieties from social change, emphasizing the need for a cultural perspective in branding strategies rather than conventional methods.

Adopting broader, more holistic perspectives that synthesize the multidimensionality of brand knowledge is critical to advance branding theory and practice, particularly in the context of brand leveraging.
The Brand Concept Maps methodology effectively identifies brand association networks and demonstrates reliability and validity through two studies.

The heterogeneity of extant research on personal branding impedes theoretical and empirical advancement, and a systematic review can strengthen the foundation for future work by proposing an integrative model that outlines trends, drivers, processes, and outcomes of personal branding.

The paper develops a framework that explains and aligns the drivers of corporate brand and corporate reputation, demonstrating that while the management and measurement of corporate identity, corporate brand, and corporate reputation may overlap, the constructs themselves are not interchangeable.

This chapter critiques existing brand research traditions and argues for a sociological conception of brands, highlighting the need for empirical research that connects society, politics, and markets.

The paper proposes a new six-step model for building strong nation brands, integrating branding literature to deepen understanding and develop action plans, while identifying five key challenges faced by nation brand marketers.

The study explores the effectiveness of three methods—free association, storytelling, and collage-creation—in eliciting consumer brand knowledge, providing insights for brand management on when to use each method.
Brand schematicity influences the role of brand concept consistency in brand extension evaluations, suggesting a moderating role in various brand-related relationships.

This study builds a dialectical theory of consumer culture and branding that explains the rise of an emerging countercultural movement against brands and projects that new contradictions will give rise to a new post-postmodern branding paradigm premised upon brands as citizen-artists.

The study affirms 13 of the 15 brand relationships detailed by Fournier and identifies three new relationships, indicating the fluidity of the consumer-brand typology across market segments and sociocultural contexts.

This study shows that consumer-made selfies shared on social media can destabilize brand assemblages by intersecting brands and branded selves through heterotopian selfie practices, influenced by the attention economy and consumer microcelebrity.

This paper aims to broaden the understanding of corporate brand associations and their transfer within the firm’s brand and product portfolio, examining the influence of corporate brand innovativeness and brand architecture as supportive and restrictive boundary conditions for its transfer.

The framework of brand concept management (BCM) significantly enhances a brand's market performance by maintaining the linkage between brand concept and brand image through a sequential process of selecting, introducing, elaborating, and fortifying the brand concept.

This research presents a new approach for harvesting qualitative information on brands from user-generated social tags, demonstrating how marketers can utilize this information to extract key topics, monitor trends, and understand perceptions of a brand.

This study discusses the main issues in managing brand equity, analyzes the concept of brand equity, provides a framework for managing it, and distinguishes ways to leverage and measure brand equity, indicating that brand equity can create advantages for firms, trade, or consumers.

The study argues for the validity of the relationship proposition in the consumer-brand context, provides a framework for understanding consumer-brand relationships, and inducts the concept of brand relationship quality as a tool for evaluating relationship strength, urging researchers to refine and test these working hypotheses.

The study explores the various aspects of strategic brand management, including building, measuring, and managing brand equity through customer-based brand equity, brand positioning, and marketing programs.

This article explores the characteristics and processes of brand communities, suggesting that these communities exhibit shared consciousness, rituals and traditions, and a sense of moral responsibility, influenced by their commercial and mass-mediated context.

This article presents a framework for measuring brand equity through various metrics, indicating that employing these measures can provide managers with useful tools despite the challenges in their application.

This research aims to develop a conceptual model of CEO brands and examines the implications of misalignment between CEO brands and company brands, suggesting that CEO brands can enhance corporate brand equity when properly aligned.

The consumer-brand relationship (CBR) domain is reflected by 71 constructs, with less than half considered important, and the study identifies seven major research streams and their emotional features, outlining insights for future research opportunities.

Social media has transformed branding by weakening traditional branding techniques and promoting cultural branding through crowdcultures, which are influential in producing content and shaping consumer perceptions.
The article discusses brand marketing big data and social innovation as significant future research directions for enhancing engagement in marketing practices.

This article investigates the general principles and specific rules of Chinese branding, revealing that branding success is influenced by syllable structure, tonic structure, compounding structure, semantic structure, and that the semantic field differentiates branding across product categories.
Consumers with a high degree of self‐brand connection respond negatively to brand developments that change brand meaning due to a change in the identity signaled by the brand.

The article presents a service-branding model that emphasizes the importance of customers' service experiences in brand formation and identifies four primary strategies that successful service firms use to cultivate brand equity.
The book illustrates the variety of ways that creativity can be embedded into a brand's communication relationships with consumers.

The interdependent relationship between person and brand can be managed through consistency and balance, with factors like mortality, hubris, unpredictability, and social embeddedness influencing brand value.

The study proposes that brand reputation, brand extension fit, and brand extension benefit innovativeness jointly impact the positive outcomes of brand extensions, with distinct strategies for strong and weak reputation brands based on the fit of the extension.

Strong brand-building skills are essential for survival and prosperity in hostile industries, and effective brand management requires consideration of emotional benefits, brand identity, and the interconnectedness of brands within a larger system.

The article proposes a stronger conceptualization of organizational identity that distinguishes it from related concepts like organizational culture and image, emphasizing its defined construct and its parallel functions to individual identity, and recommends studying member-agents' responses to the question, “Who are we as an organization?” within a defined identity-claim conceptual domain and associated phenomenological markers of identity-referencing discourse.

The construction of identity scholarship in organization studies is influenced by the durability of identity and its various conceptualizations, which provide insights into organizational settings and phenomena across different levels.

The proposed structure of abstract brand concepts as representations of human values is particularly useful for predicting brand meanings that are compatible with each other, brand concepts that resonate with consumers from differing cultural orientations, and consumers’ responses to attempts to imbue established brand concepts with new meanings based on their cultural orientations.

The enactment of IT affordances within interorganizational contexts enables new forms of social exchanges, which in turn provide the context for the rearticulation of the company's identity orientation.

This article proposes a "social actor" conception of organizational identity, clarifies the conceptual domains of organizational identity, image, and reputation, and identifies a structural flaw in the organizational reputation literature along with suitable remedies.
Brand managers must establish a frame of reference, leverage points of parity, and ensure points of difference are compelling to sustain their brand against competitors.

The study introduces privacy-friendly methods for extracting quasi-social networks from browser behavior, showing that audiences with high brand proximity exhibit substantially higher brand affinity, and provides evidence that the quasi-social network reflects a true social network, contributing to increased brand affinity.

The implementation of self-branding builds on and reaffirms a distinction between “self”—presented as unique, intimate, and real, and “market”—presented as competitive, instrumental, and unauthentic, indicating a shift in professional identities towards reliability and authenticity of display.

This article demonstrates how the nicknaming practices of Asian American youth, through brand personification and emblematic scales, create informal speech and relationships that reflect and transgress racial boundaries, while also contributing to structural change over time.

Consumers purchase brands to construct self-concepts and form self-brand connections, with brands aligned with ingroup images enhancing these connections, while outgroup images negatively affect independent consumers more than interdependent consumers due to self-differentiation goals. Greater effects are observed for symbolic brands.

The paper suggests that the cultural logic of the Celebrity Brand operates through mediated spectacles that facilitate social invention and transformation, with celebrity brands acting as map-making devices that situate consumers within networks of symbolic resources.

The data show that brand authenticity positively impacts brand trust, and the key antecedents (consistency, continuity, and individuality of a brand) drive the perception of brand authenticity as hypothesized.

Brand innovativeness and brand self-relevance have a greater effect on brand commitment in individualist, short-term oriented, and low power distance cultures, while brand customer orientation and social responsibility have a greater impact in collectivist, long-term oriented, and high power distance cultures. In collectivist, long-term-oriented, and high-power-distance cultures, the four brand management activities equally contribute to brand commitment.

This article suggests that brands acquire cultural meaning through complex processes influenced by cultural models, motivational underpinnings, shared versus idiosyncratic meanings, category-level versus brand-level meaning making, and consumer co-creation of brand meaning.

The study aims to provide hotel brand managers with a robust measure to evaluate brand equity as an outcome of brand strategies and to gain insight into the factors contributing to hotel brand equity, utilizing a quantitative methodology and Berry's service-branding model.

The proposed audience selection framework for online brand advertising significantly improves the identification of target audiences for focal brands by analyzing implicit brand-brand networks and utilizing a combination of text and network analyses.

given_idea: The lack of widespread adoption of robust measurement techniques by UK advertisers may be attributed to the tendency of government ads to diffuse responsibility, which can be mitigated by TV advertising with COVID-19 narratives that emphasize individual ownership and clear messaging.
original: TV advertising with COVID-19 narratives positively influences social distancing behavior, especially in the absence of government policy interventions, while government ads have a nonsignificant overall impact but show a significant negative effect in the presence of such interventions.

The experimental techniques for the robust measurement of ad effectiveness proposed in Gordon et al. have not been widely adopted by U.K. advertisers due to internal frictions rather than lack of access to data or deliberate actions by digital advertising platforms.

top_k.sentence: TV advertising with COVID-19 narratives positively influences social distancing behavior, especially in the absence of government policy interventions, while government ads have a nonsignificant overall impact but show a significant negative effect in the presence of such interventions.

The experimental techniques for the robust measurement of ad effectiveness proposed in Gordon et al. have not been widely adopted by U.K. advertisers due to internal frictions rather than lack of access to data or deliberate actions by digital advertising platforms.

The study investigates the reliability of measuring advertising effectiveness and the causal effects of advertising on consumer behaviors, particularly purchases, using new metrics and methods in online advertising.

The average short-term advertising elasticity is .12, which is lower than the prior mean of .22, and advertising elasticity varies based on the type of goods, stage of the product life cycle, frequency of data, and measurement method, with long-term elasticity averaging .24, lower than the prior mean of .41.

The study aims to provide insights into how national advertisers measure advertising results across various expenditure levels and categories of goods and services, based on case histories from multiple firms.

The mean long-term shares of the total effect for personal selling and targeted advertising are distinctly larger than that for mass media advertising, with corresponding median 90% implied duration intervals differing significantly among these marketing communication methods.

The article explores the implications of advertising's functions on the measurements of advertising effectiveness, suggesting that a technique-oriented approach may not adequately capture these implications.

The study reveals that TV advertising has a causal sales effect on a large number of products, with generalizable results indicating smaller advertising elasticities and considerable over-investment in advertising at the margin.

The study finds that the dynamics of attention to TV commercials are characterized by non-monotonic baseline hazard rates and considerable heterogeneity in zapping behavior across different ads and households, with no firm links established between ad characteristics and ad heterogeneity.

The study finds that systematic variability in short-term advertising elasticities is related to model specification, estimation, measurement, product type, and study setting, while the impact of advertising on sales is difficult to identify due to high sharing of explained variance among explanatory factors in quasi-experimental designs.

This article identifies four market inefficiencies in digital advertising—ad effect measurement, frictions between and within advertising channel members, ad blocking, and ad fraud—that are poorly understood and manifest uniquely in digital ad markets.
The proposed model shows that pulsing advertising strategies can generate greater total awareness than continuous advertising when ad quality varies over time due to wearout and restoration effects.

Exposure to advertising increases consumers’ tendency to purchase the promoted product by reducing the risk associated with the product for risk-averse consumers, and the effectiveness of advertising depends on various factors such as risk preference, precision of the message, consumer familiarity, sensitivity to product attributes, and product diversity.
The average advertising duration interval is of brief duration, typically between six and nine months, after adjusting for aggregation bias.

Online display advertising experiments reveal challenges such as statistical power and compliance, while also highlighting new issues like identity fragmentation and market spillovers, emphasizing the need for large-scale experiments due to tiny ad effects.
This paper analyzes how political advertising can provide exogenous variation in product advertising and under what conditions this is effective.
A probabilistic model relates advertising schedule variables to audience exposure patterns, indicating that specific schedule variables can predict the level of audience exposure to TV advertising.

The probability of zapping a commercial is influenced by factors such as previous exposures to the commercial, household purchasing behavior, and the placement of ads, with specific implications for advertising strategies.

The effects of advertising context, defined by Kelley's attribution variables (consensus, consistency over time, consistency over modality, and distinctiveness), influence the perception of an advertisement.

The absence of fake news advertising leads to a significant decline in consumer interest, as evidenced by a 22% increase in the probability of product sites not receiving new visits after the shutdown of fake news ads.

The study finds that consumers in the U.S. pay television service market are sensitive to advertising, particularly that of satellite operators, with ad elasticities of about .05--.06 for satellite operators compared to .02 for cable operators, and that asymmetric cost efficiencies in television advertising have benefited satellite operators more than cable operators, highlighting the interaction between advertising efficiencies and market competition.

Inaccurate data significantly impacts the estimation of the time-varying reproduction number R(t), which affects the evaluation of nonpharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) and the decisions made by governments during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Companies should avoid advertising during major sports events due to diminished own-advertising effectiveness and negative category-demand effects, while greater increases in advertising spending around focused, single-sport events can lead to significant growth in share of voice.

Advertising avoidance is negatively associated with movie ads and positively associated with advertising for websites, auto insurance, and women's clothing, with additional influences from repeated exposures, advertising on general-interest networks, later evening hours, and rainfall.

The proposed framework for estimating causal effects in online actions, particularly in display advertising through ad viewability as a natural experiment, significantly reduces bias in estimating campaign lift to less than 10%.
Typical ads communicate their gist effectively in adverse exposure conditions, leading to higher immediate interest compared to atypical ads after brief exposures.

Accurate and near real-time data about the trajectory of the COVID-19 pandemic are crucial for informing mitigation policies, but biases in data interpretation can lead to serious misinterpretations that affect policy decisions.

Firms that target advertising at a higher level of aggregation than their predictions allow generate advertising spillovers that are quasi-random and can be used to identify the response to advertising, which may produce biased results in standard approaches to estimating advertising response.
Copy testing is effective, no single measure suffices for all advertisements, certain measures are more suitable for specific objectives, and ads that are liked tend to outsell those that are not.

Advertising influences media bias, with the degree of polarization depending on the heterogeneity of advertisers and their choice of media outlets, where single-homing leads to greater polarization compared to multihoming.

Comparative price advertising is a powerful advertising tool that can influence consumers' internal reference price, perceived value, price offer believability, purchase likelihood, and search intentions, but it also presents a strong opportunity for deception requiring careful management and monitoring.

The "ghost ads" methodology improves the measurement of online ad effectiveness by reducing experimentation costs, enhancing measurement precision, and demonstrating significant ad lift in website visits and purchases.

Variations in the design of online banner advertisements significantly influence advertising involvement and effectiveness, with banner ad size increasing involvement through total number of clicks but not affecting effectiveness through CTR, and the Golden Ratio playing an important role in message design effectiveness.

Observational data frequently lead to incorrect estimates of the causal effects of online advertising on user behavior due to "activity bias," which results from time-based correlations between various online activities, making it difficult to find suitable matched controls.

Exclusive advertising contracts benefit both advertisers and media outlets when commercial advertising is an important means for advertisers to inform consumers about their products, despite high levels of advertising potentially leading to fiercer product price competition and reduced profitability for both parties.

This paper presents a causally motivated methodology for conversion attribution in online advertising campaigns, arguing that standardization of attribution measurement is necessary and proposing two approximation methods for causal estimation when full causal estimation cannot be performed.

The paper argues that many empirical findings regarding advertising repetition that appear contradictory are actually complementary, suggesting that the ambiguity in the literature regarding the value of advertising repetition can be resolved through a critical review of existing studies.

The study finds that cross effects exist between online and offline advertising, with online display and search advertising being more effective than traditional advertising, primarily due to strong cross effects on the offline channel, and that traditional advertising decreases paid search click-through rates, reducing the net cross effect of traditional advertising.

The study demonstrates a trade-off between advertising effectiveness and annoyance, revealing that while display advertising can positively influence purchase decisions, excessive exposure can lead to increased consumer annoyance, which varies based on the consumer's stage in the purchase funnel and is moderated by ad format and consumer demographics.

The model suggests that monopolistic newspapers under-report or bias news to reduce advertiser profits, but increasing advertising size can lead to reduced advertiser bias among competing newspapers, although advertisers can counteract this by committing to news-sensitive strategies.

Government restrictions on selective advertising lead consumers to seek information from alternative sources, such as user-generated content and unregulated medical treatment websites, after the removal of pharmaceutical ads.

The study proposes a statistically sound randomization-based methodology for measuring the causal effectiveness of digital advertising campaigns, addressing complications such as identifier instability and interference among observations that impair accurate lift measurements.

The digital video recorder (DVR) increases commercial avoidance among viewers but also facilitates the measurement of this avoidance, leading to more relevant and engaging advertising that reduces viewers' motivation to avoid ads.

Aggregate advertising models reveal that product sales respond to advertising spending in complex ways, including varying rates of response, the possibility of positive sales at zero advertising, and the influence of competitive advertising, indicating a need for models that encompass these phenomena.

This study builds a comprehensive theoretical model explaining advertising avoidance on the Internet, finding that perceived goal impediment, perceived ad clutter, and prior negative experience significantly explain why people avoid advertising messages, with perceived goal impediment being the most significant antecedent.

Ad transparency impacts ad effectiveness by revealing unacceptable information flows, which reduces effectiveness due to increased consumer privacy concerns, while platform trust moderates this effect.
A pulsed advertising policy is superior to an even policy in generating awareness for new products, as analyzed through an analytical model and applied to Zielske's data.

Exposure to advertising decreases the consumer's tendency to purchase the promoted product and improves the matching of consumers and products, with empirical support indicating that exposure to a single advertisement decreases the probability of not choosing the best alternative by at least 16%.

Advertising expenditures have a dynamic effect on consumer awareness of products, which increases present and future sales, and the length of the awareness process varies significantly among products based on advertising levels.

The growth in online newspaper advertising exacerbated the overall decline in print advertising, suggesting that advertisers perceive print and online newspaper advertising as substitutes. However, cannibalization within the newspaper is not solely responsible for the downward trajectory of print advertising.

given_idea: The study develops a theory of product-form strategy for commercializing technological innovations, identifying the primary drivers of the choice to market innovations as know-how, components, or systems, and finds consistent support for this theory across multiple studies.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: A market orientation facilitates technology-based innovations but inhibits market-based innovations, while a technology orientation benefits technology-based innovations and an entrepreneurial orientation facilitates both types of breakthroughs; different market forces significantly influence these innovations and their impact on firm performance.

The success potential of new products is significantly influenced by the product idea itself and the circumstances of its emergence, including how ideas originated, their configurations, and the technology level required for implementation.

Incumbents' research investments in radical technologies can translate into product development depending on the conformity of these technologies to existing business models and the modes of investment, which can either mitigate or exacerbate organizational inertia.

The commercial success of platform technologies is influenced by product versioning strategies, market size, and the uncertainty of developer participation, with established firms favoring expanded product lines and startups benefiting from a deferred expansion strategy.

The study finds strong evidence that both architectural and specialized marketing capabilities, and their integration, positively mediate the relationship between product-market strategy and business unit performance, specifically market effectiveness and cash flow performance.

The review identifies gaps in the literature on product design and suggests future research opportunities based on current and emerging industry trends, which may improve the impact of future research on knowledge development and industry practice.
New technologies in marketing lead to innovations in marketing techniques, tools, and strategies, providing firms that adopt them with a competitive advantage over those that do not.

The competitive interaction between start-up innovators and established firms is influenced by the presence or absence of a "market for ideas," which affects their commercialization strategies and the dynamics of industrial competition.

Japanese new product success is positively influenced by cross-functional integration, information sharing, marketing and technical resources, proficiency in new product development activities, and market conditions, with cross-functional integration and product competitive advantage being key determinants.

By combining the logics of industrial manufacturing and cultural production, organizations can create new market opportunities and enact innovation in product design through strategic recombinant strategies that restructure organizational agency.

The novel theory introduced in this article theorizes market evolution as resulting from practice expansion, which is influenced by ongoing institutional complexity and contributes to increased product diversity, broadened skills and knowledge, and an enriched market meaning system.
The identification and application of five templates derived from historical product trends can improve new product ideation and predict new product emergence.

The knowledge creation process influences sustainable competitive advantage only through the mediating effect of technological innovation capabilities, which include process innovation capability and product innovation capability.

Market orientation, specifically customer and competitor orientations, enables firms to simultaneously exploit existing product innovation competencies and explore new ones, which differentially affects incremental and radical innovation performance, with interfunctional coordination moderating these relationships.

The authors propose a conceptual model of product design and offer definitions for product design and the product design process, while analyzing publication trends and the relationship of product design research to related marketing topics during the period 1995-2008.

The strategic orientation of the firm significantly impacts new product performance, with technological orientation being superior for innovation, competitive orientation being beneficial in high-growth markets, and a combination of consumer and technology orientation leading to better performance in uncertain demand markets.

The study identifies eight features of technology-intensive markets and explores four marketing decisions, including vertical positioning, product design, transfer rights, and migration decisions, which are critical for firms operating in these markets.

This paper presents a new conceptual framework for marketers to create customer value by distinguishing themselves in the marketplace, which can be applied in designing marketing strategies, recognizing new product opportunities, and enhancing product concept specifications.

The article discusses the complex means by which consumers respond to major innovations in the marketplace, influenced by technology, cultural shifts, and social systems, highlighting the importance of understanding consumer behavior in the context of innovation diffusion.
The authors aim to clarify the meanings and uses of market segmentation and product differentiation through traditional and contemporary economic theory and product preference maps.

This paper proposes a methodology for measuring product innovations through a value metric, equating the magnitude of innovations with the welfare gains they generate, specifically applied to computed tomography scanners, and explores the social rate of return to R&D, the interrelation between innovation and diffusion, and the time profile of benefits and costs.

Market driving companies, characterized by radical innovation and unique business systems, achieve a sustainable competitive advantage over market driven companies, which primarily generate incremental innovation. Established companies face significant obstacles in adopting market driving strategies but can overcome these challenges with targeted recommendations.

The analysis shows that product innovation varies with market orientation, specifically that customer orientation increases the introduction of new-to-the-world products and reduces the launching of me-too products, competitor orientation increases the introduction of me-too products and reduces the launching of line extensions and new-to-the-world products, and interfunctional coordination increases the launching of line extensions and reduces the introduction of me-too products.

The study identifies 16 topics relevant to marketing science across five research fields related to innovation, highlighting the need for an integrative review of these research traditions and summarizing key concepts and research challenges.

Developers can achieve the innovation sweet spot by applying five innovation patterns that manipulate existing components of a product and its environment to create feasible and appealing new products.
Successful innovation requires understanding the causes of consumer resistance and developing a marketing strategy to address functional and psychological barriers.

The article argues that markets are constituted by practice and emphasizes the role of ideas in the making of markets, proposing a conceptual model that includes normalizing, representational, and exchange practices as interlinked types of practices that continuously realize markets.
Public firms use an innovation ratchet strategy to enhance stock market valuations, but this strategy results in lower sales growth in product markets due to delayed product introductions.

The key to success for companies in the age of digital convergence will be to develop innovative products and services by creatively combining existing technologies with new managerial approaches, rather than relying on major technological breakthroughs or grand acquisitions.
Corporate culture is the strongest driver of radical innovation across nations, and the commercialization of radical innovations is a stronger predictor of financial performance than patents.
The lead user idea-generation process improves innovation capabilities at 3M, resulting in significantly higher projected sales and product line generation compared to traditional methods.
Firm R&D and advertising are associated with subsequent differentiation from competitors, supporting theories of endogenous product differentiation.

The portfolios of upstream interfirm agreements in technology-intensive markets have significant consequences for radical innovation, incremental innovation, and profitability, as supported by empirical evidence from the pharmaceutical industry.

This paper empirically tests three sources of long-term pioneering cost advantage (experience curve effects, preemption of input factors, and preemption of ideal market space) and three sources of pioneering cost disadvantage (imitation, vintage effects, and demand orientation), finding support for five of the six sources in both consumer goods and industrial goods samples, while highlighting the need for managers to consider specific conditions before assuming cost implications of a pioneering strategy.

The marketing capability of a firm significantly influences its financial performance in high-technology markets, particularly when combined with strong R&D capabilities, and that the interaction between marketing and R&D capabilities is the most important determinant of a firm's performance.

The study emphasizes the importance of total design management in successful product design, highlighting the need for effective design processes and market research to enhance product definition and management.

Integration of process and product components within a firm's innovation portfolio increases firm returns, but this effect diminishes with greater production capacity or market access, and high integration lowers innovative productivity.

Firms' ability to innovate through technological recombination is driven by two distinct types of recombinant capabilities: recombinant creation, which involves creating new technological combinations, and recombinant reuse, which involves reconfiguring existing combinations. The degree of integration of a firm's intraorganizational network and the diversity of its knowledge base influence these capabilities, with integrated networks favoring recombinant creation and diverse knowledge bases enhancing both capabilities.

The dual impact of modularity on innovation and imitation creates a trade-off between innovation benefits and imitation deterrence, with design complexity influencing performance gains through innovation and the preservation of performance differences against imitation.

This paper indicates that diffusion research has significantly contributed to understanding new product adoption and highlights the importance of social structure in the communication process, while also identifying future research priorities in this area.

The proposed hypotheses focus on the methods for achieving functional integration in the R&D-marketing interface, emphasizing that quality function deployment improves information sharing and that senior management involvement is necessary for these benefits, suggesting that future research should explore both situational and structural/process dimensions.

Innovative culture is the fundamental antecedent of effective marketing strategy making (MSM), and the components of MSM have differential effects on strategy creativity, organizational learning, and market performance, with strategy creativity affecting market performance and organizational learning directly and as a mediator variable.

Knowledge in new product development serves as both a barrier to and a source of innovation, with the use of boundary objects facilitating the resolution of problematic knowledge boundaries across functions.

The analysis of firms from a resource perspective, using concepts like resource position barriers and resource-product matrices, reveals new strategic options that differ from traditional product-side analysis.
Innovative late entrants can outsell pioneers in certain markets due to specific mechanisms that drive their success.

The fragmented nature of the literature on marketing in computer-mediated environments hinders long-term knowledge development, and a proposed framework structured around four key interactions can help identify gaps and advance knowledge in this area.

The willingness to cannibalize is a more powerful driver of radical product innovation than firm size, suggesting a need to reconsider conventional wisdom on firm size, cannibalization, and organizational synergy.

The debate on the standardization versus adaptation of international marketing strategy remains unresolved due to the lack of a strong underlying theoretical framework in scholarly research over the past 40 years.

Product innovativeness consists of three separate dimensions that exhibit no or moderate correlations with product advantage, while product advantage positively and marketing discontinuity negatively influence product financial performance; project protocols positively impact product financial performance indirectly through product advantage and marketing discontinuity.

The rate of imitation of new techniques among firms is influenced by various factors, and both deterministic and stochastic models can effectively explain the differences in the rate of imitation across different innovations.

given_idea: By signaling expertise in health care delivery, hospitals can foster trust among patients, which is particularly important when patients rely on online reviews to gauge physician quality and make informed choices.
original: The hospital's portfolio strategy, characterized by both depth (focus) and breadth (related focus), positively influences patient choice by signaling expertise in health care delivery, with potential synergistic effects and heterogeneity across departments, payer types, and hospital profit status.

Online physician ratings positively influence patients' physician choices by signaling physician quality, with patients responding most to information about physicians' interpersonal and clinical skills, and the effects being moderated by rating credibility, accessibility, and strength of other signals.

top_k.sentence: Online physician ratings positively influence patients' physician choices by signaling physician quality, with patients responding most to information about physicians' interpersonal and clinical skills, and the effects being moderated by rating credibility, accessibility, and strength of other signals.
Most patients give physicians a favorable rating on online physician-rating sites, and a single overall rating may be sufficient to assess a patient's opinion of the physician.

Online ratings reflect patients' opinions about physician quality, with lower-rated physicians being less likely to receive online ratings, and online ratings being positively correlated with patient population opinions but exaggerated at the upper end of the quality spectrum.
Patients are more likely to trust and follow medical recommendations from physicians than from computer programs, and the feeling of responsibility influences this inclination.
Disclosures of specialty bias increase patients' trust and their likelihood of choosing a treatment in accordance with the physicians' specialty.
Patients who have been with their physician for at least 1 year write positive reviews on public websites and focus on physician attributes.

Hospitals can influence physician behavior and counter supplier power through strategies like the formulary and payment-cap models, with the payment-cap model being more feasible due to its preservation of physician choice and the orchestration of clinical improvements fostering trust.

This paper shows that incorporating textual information from online reviews into an econometric model significantly improves the predictive power of patient choice in healthcare delivery, with a 6%-12% improvement in mean squared error for both in-sample and out-of-sample tests. Additionally, contextual descriptions may better characterize perceived quality than numerical ratings.
